w:r.w:t: Event-Driven • Multi-Timeframe • 5–100 ML Models • 8 Concurrent Workers
Solara AI Quant is an automated trading system. It watches the market using multiple
machine learning models simultaneously, decides when to place trades, manages all open
positions automatically, and protects profits using a built-in trailing stop system.
A human operator configures the models and risk settings — then the system runs on its own.

Solara AI Quant (SAQ) is an event-driven, multi-timeframe, multi-model algorithmic trading system built on top of MetaTrader 5. It receives market data from an MT5 Expert Advisor (EA), runs a fleet of machine learning models against that data, aggregates the resulting signals, applies risk controls, and executes trades — all automatically and without human intervention during operation.

The system is designed to scale from 2 models to 100 models without any architectural changes. Adding a new model requires editing a single configuration file and dropping a model file into a folder. No code changes are required anywhere else.
Event-driven market data ingestion from MT5 EA CSV exports
Concurrent execution of 5–100 ML models per timeframe event
Signal aggregation with conflict detection across models
Risk management enforcement before every trade
Trade execution via MT5 Python API
SQLite-based persistent state for positions and model run history
Structured audit logging for every system event
Model training and ML model development
MT5 EA development (EA is a pre-existing component)
SAQ operates within the following boundaries:
MT5 EA writes CSV files to MQL5/Files/
Instead of running on a fixed timer, SAQ only runs when there is new market data.
The MT5 EA writes a new data file every time a candle closes on a given timeframe.
SAQ detects that file being written and immediately runs the relevant models.
Think of it as: the market tells SAQ when to act — SAQ does not guess when to check.
2.1 Core Design Principle — Event-Driven, Not Timer-Driven

SAQ uses no cron jobs, no fixed-interval polling, and no sleep loops. The sole trigger for any model execution cycle is a filesystem modification event on one of the four watched CSV files. This guarantees that models run on fresh, complete data — exactly at bar close — and never on stale data from a previous bar.
The following describes the complete lifecycle of a single timeframe event — from MT5 bar close to trade execution to position protection:
MT5 bar closes on H4 timeframe
Watchdog detects: H4 CSV file modification event (OS filesystem event)
Watchdog checks: Is an H4 cycle already running?
→ YES: Log WARNING 'H4 cycle overlap detected — skipping event' [skip]
→ NO: Set H4 cycle lock = TRUE, proceed
Validate: timestamps, row count, required columns, no nulls in OHLCV
Trim to latest candle per symbol
FAIL: log error, release lock, stop cycle (do not run models on bad data)
Run UniversalPreprocessor on validated H4 DataFrame
Compute: BB bands, RSI, momentum, body size, lags [1,2,3]
Output: feature-engineered DataFrame (same row count, more columns)
Query Model Registry: get all models where timeframe == 'H4' AND enabled == true
Validate feature version compatibility for each model
Dispatch models to worker pool (max 8 concurrent)
Detect and suppress conflicting long/short signals on same symbol
Output: List[AggregatedSignal] — ready for risk check
→ FAIL any 1: log rejection reason, discard signal
Calculate lot size dynamically (equity * risk_pct / pip_value)
Place order via MT5 Python API
Retry up to MAX_RETRIES if MT5 returns retryable error code
Log result (success/failure/rejection) to audit log + SQLite TradeLog
Survivor runs on its own cycle: every SURVIVOR_INTERVAL_SECONDS
Fetches all open positions from MT5
Calculates current stage for each position based on pips in profit
Applies SL/TP modifications if stage has changed
Logs each stage transition to SQLite PositionState table
Log cycle summary: models run, signals generated, trades placed, elapsed time

Each timeframe operates as a completely independent pipeline. An M5 cycle and an H4 cycle can run simultaneously without any interaction. They share only two resources: the MT5 connection (thread-safe via a connection pool) and the SQLite database (atomic writes with row-level locking).
If a file modification event fires for a timeframe whose cycle is still running,
the new event is SKIPPED — not queued, not held.
Behavior: Log a WARNING with timestamp, timeframe, and elapsed time of the running cycle.
Release the lock only when the active cycle completes naturally.
This protects against rare edge cases (e.g. EA writing file twice, filesystem events
firing twice on some OS configurations). It does NOT happen under normal operation.
If this warning appears regularly, it indicates models are taking too long to run
and the operator should review model count or worker allocation for that timeframe.
An M15 model and an H4 model may both hold open positions on the same symbol
at the same time. They are considered entirely independent.
Each model has a unique magic number registered in model_registry.yaml.
The Survivor Engine tracks all positions by ticket + magic number.
The Risk Manager checks position limits per model (model.max_positions),
Ticket #1001 magic=201000 (BB Long H4) Stage 7 — protecting 40% profit
Ticket #1002 magic=301000 (RSI Short M15) Stage 2 — protecting 15% profit
Both are managed independently by the Survivor Engine.
The File Watchdog is the system's eyes. It continuously monitors four folders
and immediately reacts the moment the MT5 EA saves a new data file.
It is the only component that decides when the system wakes up and runs.

The File Watchdog is implemented using the Python watchdog library (watchdog>=3.0.0). It uses OS-native filesystem event APIs: inotify on Linux, ReadDirectoryChangesW on Windows. This means event detection is immediate — there is no polling delay.
# Event type ignored: FILE_CREATED, FILE_DELETED, FILE_MOVED
# Reason: EA always modifies existing file, never creates a new one per cycle
File modified, no active cycle for that TF
INFO: '{TF} cycle started — file modified at {timestamp}'
File modified, cycle already active for that TF
WARNING: '{TF} cycle overlap detected — skipping. Active cycle elapsed: {N}s'
File modified but file is empty (0 bytes)
Skip event, do not start cycle
WARNING: '{TF} CSV is empty — skipping cycle'
File modified but file fails validation
Non-watched file modified in same directory
Each timeframe maintains an independent cycle lock. Locks are implemented as threading.Event objects — one per timeframe. They are not file locks, not database locks. They exist only in memory.
# Release: cycle_locks[tf].clear() — after pipeline completes OR errors
# CRITICAL: lock MUST be released in a try/finally block.
# If the pipeline crashes, the lock is still released.
# A stuck lock would permanently block that timeframe.

Watchdog starts before any other component. It must confirm all 4 CSV files exist before starting observation. If any file is missing, log a WARNING but continue watching — the EA may not have written that timeframe yet.
On SIGTERM or SIGINT, the watchdog stops accepting new events. All active cycles are allowed to complete. System exits only when all cycle locks are released.
The watchdog process is supervised by the OS-level process manager. If it crashes, the supervisor restarts it within 10 seconds. On restart, it re-acquires the watch on all 4 files from scratch.
The Model Registry is the master list of all ML models in the system.
It is a single YAML file. Adding a new model means adding one block to this file.
No Python code needs to change anywhere. The system reads this file at startup
and validates every entry before any trading begins.
# Loaded at: system startup, before watchdog starts
# Validated by: Pydantic v2 ModelRegistryEntry schema
# Hot-reload: supported in Phase 3 — applied at next cycle boundary
- name: "BB Long Reversal v2" # str, required, unique across all entries
model_file: "BB_LONG_v2.pkl" # str, required — filename in Models/ dir
feature_version: "v3" # str, required — must match feature_versions.yaml
model_type: "LONG" # str, required — enum: LONG | SHORT
timeframe: "H4" # str, required — enum: M5 | M15 | H1 | H4
# lower number = higher priority in queue
timeout: 30 # int, required — seconds before model is
magic: 201000 # int, required — unique MT5 magic number
# NO two models may share a magic number
comment: "SAQ_BBLong" # str, required — MT5 trade comment prefix
enabled: true # bool, required — false = excluded from all cycles
max_positions: 3 # int, required — max simultaneous open positions
# empty list = trade all symbols in data
timeframe: "M15" # <-- this model runs on M15 bar close only
Startup FATAL: duplicate or missing name. System will not start.
Startup FATAL: import attempted at load time. Fails if class not found.
Startup FATAL: if enabled=true and file missing, entry rejected.
Startup FATAL: version mismatch causes entry rejection.
Startup FATAL: invalid value causes entry rejection.
Startup FATAL: unknown timeframe causes entry rejection.
Startup FATAL: out of range causes entry rejection.
Startup FATAL: out of range causes entry rejection.
Startup FATAL: out of range causes entry rejection.
Startup FATAL: out of range causes entry rejection.
Startup FATAL: duplicate magic number causes system halt.
Startup FATAL: out of range causes entry rejection.
Valid MT5 symbol names or empty
WARNING at startup if symbol not found in MT5. Model still runs.
If false, entry is loaded but excluded from all execution queues.
Magic numbers are the primary identifier linking a trade to its originating model. The following convention is recommended but not enforced by the system — the system only enforces uniqueness:
Each timeframe has its own complete pipeline — from reading the CSV file
to computing features, running models, and placing trades.
The four pipelines (M5, M15, H1, H4) are structurally identical but operate
on different data files and trigger different sets of models.
They run fully independently — they never wait for each other.
Feature Engineering runs ONCE per timeframe event. The result is a single
fully-featured DataFrame shared across all models on that timeframe.
No model computes its own features — they all consume the same pre-computed result.

All four timeframe pipelines run in true parallel. Each pipeline lives in its own Python thread, managed by the watchdog's event dispatch system. When M5, M15, H1, and H4 all fire at the same bar boundary — which occurs every hour when all four align — all four pipelines execute simultaneously with zero coordination between them.
# Every 15 minutes: M5 + M15 fire simultaneously
# Every 60 minutes: M5 + M15 + H1 fire simultaneously
# Every 4 hours: M5 + M15 + H1 + H4 fire simultaneously ← all 4 pipelines
# Thread A: M5 pipeline → M5 feature engineering → M5 models (up to 8 concurrent)
# Thread B: M15 pipeline → M15 feature engineering → M15 models (up to 8 concurrent)
# Thread C: H1 pipeline → H1 feature engineering → H1 models (up to 8 concurrent)
# Thread D: H4 pipeline → H4 feature engineering → H4 models (up to 8 concurrent)
# All 4 threads run at the same time. Total concurrent models: up to 32.
# Each pipeline's worker pool is independent (8 workers each).
# Shared resources (MT5 connection, SQLite DB) are thread-safe.
No pipeline waits for another. An H4 pipeline that takes 60 seconds to run
all its models does not delay or block the M5 pipeline running next to it.
The only shared resources between pipelines are:
- SQLite database (atomic row-level writes via SQLAlchemy)
Everything else — data, features, model queue, worker pool — is private to each pipeline.

The MT5 EA exports one CSV file per timeframe to the MT5 terminal's MQL5/Files/ directory on every bar close. The CSV contains raw OHLCV data and a small number of price-derived columns. It does NOT contain computed indicators or features — all indicator computation is SAQ's responsibility.
MT5 EA CSV Schema — Exact Columns Exported
# These are the ONLY columns the MT5 EA provides.
# SAQ must compute ALL features from these inputs.
timestamp datetime Bar open time. Format: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS
symbol str MT5 symbol name. e.g. 'EURUSD', 'XAUUSD'
high float Bar high price (highest tick of the bar)
low float Bar low price (lowest tick of the bar)
tick_volume int Number of ticks during the bar
spread int Spread in points at bar close
price float Current bid price at moment of CSV export
# NOTE: The CSV contains MULTIPLE bars per symbol (lookback window).
# SAQ needs historical rows to compute lagged features and rolling indicators.
# The EA exports the last N bars (e.g. N=100) for each symbol per file.
File must not be empty (0 rows after header). Empty file → skip cycle, log WARNING.
All 9 required columns must be present and correctly named. Missing column → abort cycle, log ERROR.
No null/NaN values in open, high, low, close, symbol columns. Rows with nulls in OHLC → drop row, log WARNING. If ALL rows are null → abort cycle.
high >= low for every row. Violated rows are dropped with WARNING. Inverted bars indicate corrupt EA export.
timestamp column must be parseable as datetime. Unparseable rows → drop. If no parseable timestamps → abort.
Minimum lookback rows per symbol: 30 bars. If fewer than 30 bars exist for a symbol, that symbol is excluded from this cycle for all models. Log WARNING per excluded symbol.
After all other validation, sort each symbol's rows by timestamp ascending before feature computation.
Feature Engineering transforms the raw OHLCV data from the MT5 EA into a rich
set of computed indicators that ML models use to make predictions.
This runs ONCE per timeframe per cycle. The resulting DataFrame is shared
read-only across all models on that timeframe — no model recomputes features.
All features are computed per-symbol using that symbol's full lookback history.
The final output is trimmed to the LATEST bar only per symbol (1 row per symbol)
before being passed to the model fleet.
Features must be computed in this exact order. Some features depend on earlier ones.
candle_body_pct = body_size / candle_range # guard: if range==0, set to 0.0
rs = avg_gain / avg_loss # guard: loss==0 → rs=inf
dist_bb_upper = close - bb_upper # negative = below upper band
dist_bb_lower = close - bb_lower # positive = above lower band
# Keep only the LATEST bar per symbol (last row after sort by timestamp asc)
# Drop all historical rows — models only see the current bar's features
# Result: 1 row per symbol, N feature columns
After all computation stages, the Feature Engineer produces a DataFrame with the following columns. This is the exact schema passed to every model.
Bar open time — passed through unchanged
abs(close - open) — absolute candle body size
body_size / (high - low). 0.0 if range is zero
close - bb_upper (negative = price below upper band)
close - bb_lower (positive = price above lower band)
5.3.3 Shared DataFrame — How Models Consume Features

After feature engineering completes, the resulting DataFrame is a single in-memory object. It is passed to the Model Execution Engine as a read-only shared reference. Every model in the timeframe fleet receives a copy of this DataFrame — not a re-computation of it.
featured_df = FeatureEngineer.compute(validated_df) # one computation, one result
# 2. Engine passes a copy to each model thread:
featured_df.copy() # shallow copy — each thread gets its own reference
) # copy() prevents any model from mutating shared state
# 3. Each model selects only the columns it needs:
signal = model.predict(model_input) # inference on selected features
# Cost: one .copy() per model per cycle.
# For 8 models on a DataFrame with 30 symbols x 27 columns:
# Memory overhead per copy: ~65KB. 8 copies: ~520KB. Negligible.

Before the Model Execution Engine dispatches any model, the Feature Validator checks that the feature_version declared in the model's registry entry is compatible with the features actually produced this cycle. This runs before the worker pool is engaged.
# feature_versions.yaml — defines which columns belong to each version
columns: [ret, ret_lag1, ret_lag2, ret_lag3, body_size, candle_body_pct,
missing = [f for f in required if f not in featured_df.columns]
Feature version mismatch is a SILENT killer — a model that runs on the wrong
features produces invalid predictions without raising an exception.
The Feature Validator catches this BEFORE the model runs, not after.
Any model with a missing feature is skipped entirely for that cycle.
The Model Execution Engine is the powerhouse of Solara AI Quant.
It takes a list of models assigned to a timeframe, runs up to 8 simultaneously,
handles failures so one broken model never affects others,
and tracks the health of every model over time.
Models within a timeframe run as concurrent threads.
Across all four timeframes, pipelines run as truly parallel threads.
The result: in a busy cycle, up to 32 models can be running at the same time.
SAQ uses a two-level concurrency model. Understanding both levels is essential for grasping how the system achieves speed at scale.
# Each timeframe pipeline runs in its own dedicated OS thread.
# These threads are spawned by the File Watchdog when a CSV event fires.
# They run in TRUE parallel — the OS scheduler assigns them to CPU cores.
# OS Thread 1 → M5 Pipeline [Feature Eng → Model Queue → Worker Pool]
# OS Thread 2 → M15 Pipeline [Feature Eng → Model Queue → Worker Pool]
# OS Thread 3 → H1 Pipeline [Feature Eng → Model Queue → Worker Pool]
# OS Thread 4 → H4 Pipeline [Feature Eng → Model Queue → Worker Pool]
# Within each pipeline, models run as concurrent worker threads.
# Max 8 workers per pipeline. Models beyond 8 queue and wait for a free slot.
# 4 pipelines × 8 workers = 32 models running at the exact same moment.
# This is the maximum concurrency of Solara AI Quant at full load.
This is a deliberate architectural choice. The decision favors ThreadPoolExecutor for model inference. Here is the full reasoning:
Shared memory space — featured DataFrame passed by reference, zero serialization cost
Each process gets its own memory — DataFrame must be pickled and sent to each process on every cycle
Models loaded once at startup, shared across all threads in same pipeline
Each process must reload .pkl model file from disk — 50 processes = 50 model loads per cycle
Process creation: 100–500ms per process — catastrophic for M5 cycles
GIL released during numpy/sklearn inference — threads run in near-true parallel for compute-heavy operations
No GIL concern — but overhead of process management exceeds any benefit for this workload
Single process — full stack traces, easy logging, shared state visible
Multi-process — errors in child processes harder to capture and surface
Simple, well-understood, battle-tested for ML inference workloads
Significant added complexity with no speed benefit for pickle model inference
Python's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) prevents true thread parallelism for pure Python code.
However — sklearn model inference (predict()) is primarily numpy operations.
Numpy releases the GIL during its core C-level computations.
This means: when 8 model threads call model.predict() simultaneously,
the heavy computation (matrix multiplication, decision tree traversal) runs
in actual parallel at the C level — not serialized by the GIL.
Result: ThreadPoolExecutor achieves near-true parallelism for ML inference
without the overhead and complexity of ProcessPoolExecutor.
# config.py — single source of truth for worker ceiling
# changing this to 16 = one config line change
# Implementation — one executor per pipeline thread
# - Executor created when pipeline thread starts (after file event fires)
# - Executor shut down (with wait=True) after all model batches complete
# - Worker threads are daemon threads — do not prevent clean system shutdown
# - A new executor is created for each cycle (not reused across cycles)

When a timeframe event fires, all enabled models for that timeframe are sorted into a priority queue. Models are dispatched in batches of MAX_CONCURRENT_MODELS. Each batch runs fully before the next batch starts. Within a batch, all models run simultaneously.
queue.put((model.priority, model.name, model)) # priority asc = runs first
while len(batch) < MAX_CONCURRENT_MODELS and not queue.empty():
# ── Dispatch batch — ALL models in batch start simultaneously ──
# ── Collect results — wait for ALL in batch to complete ──
# ── Next batch starts only after current batch fully completes ──
Result marked FAILED. Full traceback logged at ERROR level. Failure recorded in ModelHealth. Other models in same batch unaffected.
Future cancelled. Result marked TIMEOUT. WARNING logged. Timeout count incremented in ModelHealth.
Result marked EMPTY. INFO logged. No signal forwarded to aggregator this cycle.
Model excluded before entering queue. ERROR logged by Feature Validator. Other models run normally.
All models in a timeframe fail/timeout
CRITICAL logged. No trades placed for this cycle. Position protection continues on its own independent loop.
Pipeline thread logs CRITICAL and exits. Cycle lock released. Other timeframes continue running.
The engine maintains a health record for every model in the ModelHealth SQLite table. Records are updated (UPSERT) after every cycle completion.
# Fields updated in ModelHealth after every cycle
model_name: str # registry name — primary key
last_run_at: datetime # timestamp of last execution attempt
last_run_status: str # SUCCESS | FAILED | TIMEOUT | EMPTY
consecutive_failures: int # resets to 0 on any SUCCESS or EMPTY
total_runs: int # lifetime run count (incremented every cycle)
avg_elapsed_seconds: float # rolling average: (old_avg * (n-1) + new) / n
auto_disabled: bool # set to true by auto-disable rule
A model is automatically disabled when consecutive_failures >= 3.
1. Model's enabled status set to False IN MEMORY (registry YAML not modified).
2. Model excluded from all future cycle queues until manually re-enabled.
4. CRITICAL log entry raised: 'Model [{name}] auto-disabled — 3 consecutive failures'.
To re-enable: set enabled: true in model_registry.yaml and restart SAQ.
The YAML file is never modified automatically by the system.
EMPTY results (model ran but produced no signals) do NOT count as failures.
Only FAILED and TIMEOUT results increment consecutive_failures.
When multiple models run on the same bar, they may produce different signals
for the same symbol. The Signal Aggregator decides which signals are real enough
to act on, which ones cancel each other out, and which ones to discard.
It is the quality filter between raw model output and actual trades.
Each model produces zero or more RawSignal objects. A signal is only produced if the model's confidence meets or exceeds its min_confidence threshold.
# RawSignal — output from a single model for a single symbol
weight: float # model's weight from registry
price: float # current price at signal time

SAQ uses a per-model pass-through strategy by default. Each model's signal is evaluated independently — there is no cross-model voting required to place a trade. This is the correct design when models are trained on different strategies, features, or timeframes.
# A signal from Model A does not require confirmation from Model B.
# This allows models on different strategies to trade independently.
The Conflict Checker runs after aggregation. It detects cases where the same model is generating conflicting signals and suppresses them.
Same model, same symbol, LONG + SHORT in same cycle
SUPPRESS BOTH. Log WARNING: 'Conflicting signals from {model} on {symbol} — both suppressed.' This should not happen in a well-trained model. Investigate if seen regularly.
Different models (different magic), same symbol, LONG + SHORT
ALLOW BOTH. They are independent models with independent positions. No suppression.
Same model, same symbol, same direction — duplicate signal
ALLOW ONE, discard duplicate. Keep highest confidence. Log DEBUG.
Model produces signal for symbol not in its symbols whitelist
DISCARD. Log WARNING: 'Signal for {symbol} from {model} — symbol not in whitelist.'
confidence: float # original model confidence (not averaged)
comment: str # prefix + confidence score appended
The Risk Manager is the last checkpoint before a trade is placed.
It enforces hard limits on account risk, position count, and daily activity.
If a signal fails any check, the trade is rejected and logged — no exceptions.
These rules protect the account from runaway model behavior.
Checks run in this exact order. A signal that fails any check is immediately rejected — subsequent checks are not run.
Current account equity must be >= (initial_equity * (1 - MAX_DAILY_DRAWDOWN_PCT))
REJECT signal. Log ERROR. Halt ALL trading for remainder of session.
Trades placed today (per model magic) < MAX_DAILY_TRADES
Open positions with this model's magic < model.max_positions
REJECT signal. Log INFO: 'Position limit reached for {model}'
Available margin >= required margin for this trade (calculated from lot size)
REJECT signal. Log WARNING: 'Insufficient margin for {symbol}'
Calculated lot size > 0.0 AND <= broker's maximum lot size
REJECT signal. Log ERROR: 'Invalid lot size calculated: {lot}'
MAX_DAILY_DRAWDOWN_PCT: float = 0.05 # 5% max daily equity drawdown
# When breached: all trading halted for session
MAX_DAILY_TRADES: int = 20 # Per model magic number, per calendar day
MAX_RISK_PER_TRADE: float = 0.02 # 2% of current equity risked per trade
# Used in dynamic lot size calculation
MAX_CONCURRENT_MODELS: int = 8 # Worker pool ceiling — not a risk param
STOP_LOSS_PIPS: int = 30 # Default SL — used in lot size calc
# Per-model override: add sl_pips to registry
MAX_SLIPPAGE_POINTS: int = 10 # Max acceptable slippage on order fill
# from SymbolHelper — accounts for JPY, XAU, etc.
sl_pips = model.sl_pips if model.sl_pips else config.STOP_LOSS_PIPS
lot_size = round(lot_size, 2) # round to broker's step
lot_size = max(MIN_LOT, min(MAX_LOT, lot_size)) # clamp to broker limits
The Survivor Engine protects every open position automatically.
As a trade moves into profit, the engine locks in progressively more of that profit
by moving the stop-loss upward (for long trades) or downward (for short trades).
By Stage 22, the system is protecting 90% of the maximum profit reached.
The engine runs independently of the model pipeline — it never stops working,
even when models are running or the market is quiet.
# Survivor runs on an independent timer — not triggered by CSV events
SURVIVOR_INTERVAL_SECONDS: int = 60 # config.py — run every 60 seconds
# 1. Fetch all open positions from MT5 (all magic numbers)
# 2. For each position, calculate current pips in profit
# 3. Determine current stage based on pips in profit
# 4. If stage > previous recorded stage: apply SL/TP update
# 5. Write stage transition to SQLite PositionState table
# 6. Log all modifications to audit log
Trade just opened. SL at initial position. Full TP active.
First protection kicks in. SL moved to lock 10% of max profit.
30% locked — trade is in solid territory.
Exactly half the maximum profit is now protected.
TP removed. Pure trailing begins. 72% locked.
Peak protection. 90% of max profit locked in.
# new_sl must be STRICTLY better than current_sl (never move SL backward)
# new_sl must respect broker's minimum stop distance for the symbol
# If new_sl <= current_sl (for long): do not modify — log DEBUG
Stages only move FORWARD. A position at Stage 9 can advance to Stage 10, but can never revert to Stage 8, even if price pulls back.
The Survivor Engine only modifies the SL. It never closes positions directly.
If MT5 rejects an SL modification (e.g. stop too close), log the error and retry on next cycle. Do not advance the stage record until modification is confirmed.
Stage 14 onwards — TP is set to 0 (removed). Once removed, TP is never reinstated.
A position that closes (TP hit, SL hit, or manual close) is removed from the PositionState table and its closure is logged as an immutable event.
All persistent data in Solara AI Quant is stored in a single SQLite database file.
SQLite replaces the old JSON flat files. It supports atomic writes, row-level locking,
and is queryable — making it easy to audit, analyse, and debug the system.
The database is never directly edited by humans. All writes go through the application.
# Access: Application only — never edit directly
Tracks the current Survivor Engine stage and protection level for every open position.
ticket INTEGER NOT NULL UNIQUE, -- MT5 position ticket
magic INTEGER NOT NULL, -- links to model registry
timeframe TEXT NOT NULL, -- M5 | M15 | H1 | H4
position_type TEXT NOT NULL, -- LONG | SHORT
initial_tp REAL, -- NULL if no TP set
highest_price REAL NOT NULL, -- for LONG: highest close seen
lowest_price REAL NOT NULL, -- for SHORT: lowest close seen
opened_at TEXT NOT NULL, -- ISO8601 datetime
last_updated_at TEXT NOT NULL, -- ISO8601 datetime
closed_at TEXT, -- NULL if still open
close_reason TEXT -- SL_HIT | TP_HIT | MANUAL | UNKNOWN
Immutable append-only log of every stage change. Never updated — only inserted.
pips_in_profit REAL NOT NULL, -- at time of transition
protection_pct REAL NOT NULL, -- new protection percentage
mt5_confirmed INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 -- 1 if MT5 accepted the SL change
Records every model execution attempt — success, failure, or timeout.
cycle_triggered_at TEXT NOT NULL, -- when file watchdog fired
status TEXT NOT NULL, -- SUCCESS | FAILED | TIMEOUT | EMPTY
batch_number INTEGER NOT NULL, -- which batch this model ran in
Records every signal produced by every model — whether acted on or not.
direction TEXT NOT NULL, -- LONG | SHORT
aggregation_outcome TEXT NOT NULL, -- PASSED | SUPPRESSED_CONFLICT | BELOW_THRESHOLD
trade_ticket INTEGER, -- MT5 ticket if trade placed, else NULL
Records every trade placement attempt — successful or not.
direction TEXT NOT NULL, -- LONG | SHORT
fill_price REAL, -- NULL if not filled
ticket INTEGER, -- MT5 ticket, NULL if failed
status TEXT NOT NULL, -- PLACED | FAILED | REJECTED
attempts INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1, -- retry count
confirmed_at TEXT, -- when MT5 confirmed ticket
Per-model health record. One row per model — updated (not inserted) after each cycle.
Every file in Solara AI Quant has exactly one home — its domain folder.
The structure below is the complete codebase layout for both dev and prod.
Adding a new ML model means dropping files in two places only:
predictors/ and Models/. Everything else stays untouched.
# Entry point — boots all domains, connects MT5, starts watchdog
# Master config — all constants, paths, env var loading
# Model fleet registry — ONLY file to edit when adding a model
# Template for environment variables — commit this, never .env
# All Python dependencies with pinned versions
# Abstract base class for all data adapters
# Validates schema, nulls, OHLC integrity, min lookback rows
# Orchestrates adapter + validator, returns clean DataFrame
# Domain 2 — Feature Engineering (runs once per TF cycle)
# All 8 computation stages: returns, RSI, BB, lags, momentum
# Checks each model's feature_version against computed columns
# v1/v2/v3 column lists — controls which features each model sees
# watchdog lib observer — monitors 4 CSV files for modification
# On file event: checks lock, fires pipeline thread
# Runs full cycle: ingest → features → engine → signals → risk → execute
# Core: priority queue → batch dispatcher → ThreadPoolExecutor
# Per-model health tracking, auto-disable after 3 failures
# Assembles ModelResultSet from all batch completions
# Domain 4b — ML Strategy Classes (one file per model type)
# BB Reversal Long — CORRECTED class (was pointing to Short)
# Drop new strategies here — register in model_registry.yaml only
# Routes ModelResultSet through active aggregation strategy
# Suppresses same-model opposing signals on same symbol
# 5-check pre-trade gate: drawdown, count, positions, margin, lot
# Dynamic lot size: equity * risk_pct / (sl_pips * pip_value)
# MT5 order placement with retry + ticket confirmation
# MT5 Interface — single abstraction for all MetaTrader 5 calls
# Connection pool, order placement, position fetch, modify SL/TP
# Pip sizes, min stop distances, lot step per symbol
# Core 22-stage logic — migrated from legacy, logic preserved
# Independent timer loop — runs every SURVIVOR_INTERVAL_SECONDS
# Logs every stage transition as immutable audit event
# Persistence — SQLite replaces all legacy JSON flat files
# SQLAlchemy engine, session factory, atomic write helpers
# SQLite DB file — git-ignored, never committed
# Full pytest suite — mirrors domain structure
# Shared fixtures: mock MT5, sample DataFrame, mock registry
# Event handling, cycle lock, overlap skip
# Fault isolation, batching, timeout, health auto-disable
# Risk checks, position sizing, retry logic
# All structured events in JSON-lines format
# Append-only: trades, stage changes, model runs
# ML model .pkl files — outside Trading Bot folder (same as legacy)
# Drop new .pkl here, register in model_registry.yaml
Entry point. Initializes all domains, connects MT5, starts file watchdog and Survivor runner.
All constants, file paths, risk parameters, concurrency settings. Loads credentials from OS env vars.
The only file edited to add/remove/toggle a model. Pydantic-validated at startup.
Template showing required env vars (MT5_LOGIN, MT5_PASSWORD, MT5_SERVER). Committed to git. Actual .env is git-ignored.
Production dependencies: watchdog, pandas, scikit-learn, sqlalchemy, pydantic, MetaTrader5, pyyaml, structlog.
Reads and validates the MT5 EA CSV. Returns a clean multi-bar DataFrame per symbol ready for feature engineering.
Computes all 18 derived features from raw OHLCV in 8 ordered stages. Runs once per cycle. Result shared to all models.
Monitors 4 CSV files. Fires independent pipeline threads on modification. Manages cycle locks per timeframe.
Loads model registry, dispatches models via priority queue and ThreadPoolExecutor, collects results, tracks health.
One Python class per model type. All inherit BasePredictor. Dropped here and registered in YAML — no other changes needed.
Aggregates raw model outputs. Passes valid signals through. Suppresses same-model conflicts. Outputs AggregatedSignal list.
Risk Manager (5 pre-trade checks) + Position Sizer (dynamic lot) + Trade Executor (MT5 order + retry + confirm).
Single abstraction layer for all MT5 Python API calls. Broker or version changes → edit this folder only.
22-stage trailing stop engine. Runs on its own independent timer. Reads from MT5, writes to SQLite. Never touches the pipeline.
SQLite database via SQLAlchemy. 6 tables. Replaces all legacy JSON files. Atomic writes, row-level locking.
pytest suite mirroring every domain. conftest.py provides shared fixtures. No new feature ships without its tests.
JSON-structured logs. system.log for all events. audit.log is append-only for trades and stage transitions.
ML .pkl model files. Lives outside the Trading Bot folder, same convention as legacy Solara.
SAQ runs on two machines: a dev machine for building and testing,
and a prod machine connected to a live MT5 account.
The codebase is identical on both. The only differences are in config.
Dev model files (may be test models)
Dev database — can be deleted freely
11.4 Adding a New Model — Complete File Checklist
# Every step required to add model #N to the fleet.
# Files changed: 2 new files + 1 YAML entry.
# Files NOT changed: main.py, config.py, execution_engine.py,
Add one entry with all 14 fields (see Section 4).
STEP 4 — Restart SAQ (or hot-reload in Phase 3).
The engine picks up the new model automatically.
Startup log will confirm: 'Loaded N models for M15'
Next M15 cycle log should show the new model running.
Check ModelHealth table after first cycle for status.
Solara AI Quant — the system described in this document.
An automated script running inside MetaTrader 5 that exports market data to CSV files.
MetaTrader 5 — the trading platform SAQ connects to for market data and trade execution.
A single unit of market data representing a time period (e.g. one H4 bar = 4 hours of price action).
The duration of each bar. SAQ supports M5 (5 min), M15 (15 min), H1 (1 hour), H4 (4 hours).
The SAQ component that monitors CSV files and fires an event when any file is modified.
The YAML file (model_registry.yaml) that defines all ML models in the fleet.
A unique integer assigned to each model. MT5 uses this to tag every trade placed by that model.
One complete execution of the pipeline for a given timeframe — from data read to trade placement.
An in-memory flag that prevents a second cycle from starting while one is already running.
A pool of 8 parallel threads that run ML models concurrently.
A group of models dispatched to the worker pool simultaneously (max 8 per batch).
The design property where one model's failure does not affect any other model.
A recommendation from a model to buy (LONG) or sell (SHORT) a specific symbol.
A signal that has passed through the conflict checker and is ready for risk evaluation.
The position management system that applies the 22-stage trailing stop logic to all open trades.
One of 22 defined profit milestones in the Survivor Engine. Each stage has a protection percentage.
The fraction of the maximum reached profit that the stop-loss is set to protect.
The monetary value of one pip movement for a given symbol and lot size.
A lightweight embedded database used by SAQ for all persistent state storage.
A label (e.g. v3) that identifies which set of computed features a model was trained on.
Automatic exclusion of a model from all cycles after 3 consecutive failures.
Python concurrency tool — runs up to 8 model threads simultaneously within one pipeline.
Global Interpreter Lock — Python mechanism that is released during numpy/sklearn inference, enabling near-true thread parallelism for ML model execution.
Initial release. All 10 core sections plus glossary.
Added full Feature Engineering spec (Sec 5), ThreadPoolExecutor rationale, two-level concurrency architecture, GIL release explanation.

Added Section 11: File Architecture — full directory tree, domain-to-folder reference, dev/prod environment differences, model onboarding checklist. Fixed MS Word compatibility issue (duplicate style definitions).
SOLARA AI QUANT — FUNCTIONAL SPECIFICATION v1.2
Confidential | February 2026 | Office of Technology Strategy
